name: ntex (Rust Async Web/Messaging Framework)

performance.notes: One of the fastest Rust frameworks; often tops TechEmpower benchmarks

agent_readiness.notes: Actix-web fork focused on performance and messaging patterns. use ntex::web; #[ntex::web::get('/')] async fn index() -> impl web::Responder { web::HttpResponse::Ok().body('Hello') }. #[ntex::main] async fn main() { web::HttpServer::new(|| { web::App::new().service(index) }).bind('127.0.0.1:8080').unwrap().run().await.unwrap() }. JSON: web::types::Json<T>. State: web::types::State<T>. Routing similar to Actix-web. Also supports MQTT, WebSocket messaging protocols beyond HTTP. Custom async runtime support (ntex::rt). Pipeline/service model for middleware. Fewer learning resources than Actix-web.

gotchas: Much smaller community than Actix-web or Axum
Documentation sparse — primarily docs.rs and source
Forked from Actix-web but not API-compatible in all areas
Custom runtime (ntex::rt) differs from tokio standard
Fewer tutorials and Stack Overflow answers
best_when: Maximum raw HTTP performance in benchmarks
MQTT or messaging protocol support alongside HTTP
When Actix-web migration path is needed with performance focus
avoid_when: Community and ecosystem are priorities (use Axum or Actix-web)
summary: ntex is a performance-focused Rust web/messaging framework, forked from Actix-web. web::HttpServer with App::new().service(). JSON<T>, State<T>. Also supports MQTT and messaging protocols. Custom ntex::rt runtime. Often tops TechEmpower benchmarks. MIT. Very small community — documentation sparse. Best for raw performance needs or MQTT+HTTP combined apps. Prefer Axum or Actix-web for community support.
